rationale: Hessian Matrix is a specific matrix of second-order partial derivatives used in calculus to describe curvature, while Matrix in linear algebra is a general rectangular array representing linear transformations. They are different concepts at different granularities and contexts.

evidence_spans.span: For twice differentiable functions, a second-order approximation also includes the Hessian matrix, which describes local curvature. These approximations are useful because they turn a complicated function into a local linear or quadratic model. In optimization, this local model helps explain why the gradient gives a useful direction for changing the input.

A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers that can represent a linear transformation once bases have been chosen for the input and output spaces. Matrix multiplication corresponds to composing linear transformations, and changing a basis changes the matrix representation without changing the underlying transformation.
